title: I tried the most CONTROVERSIAL iPhone 17 Case…🥶
transcript: Okay, so a lot of people have been asking for a review of the Arc Pulse case on the 17 Pro Max. So, let's talk about it. The design of the cases here obviously accommodates for the new camera plateau across the entire top of the phone. And I'm actually really into how this looks when paired with the new design of the Pro models. Not only does the case protect the sharp edges of the camera bar, which we know is prone to chipping and scratching, but it also gives you this really nice bump to rest your finger on when holding the phone instead of always having the full weight of the device resting on your pinky finger. It's really nice. Now, one big difference with the 17 Pro models is the fact that they use aluminum for the body of the phone, which is much softer and way easier to dent or scratch compared to the titanium frame on last year's phones. So, I'm not going to lie, I am actually pretty nervous about having the edges exposed here. I actually think the perfect iPhone to pair with the Arc Pulse this year is the iPhone Air since you still have the super strong titanium frame and pairing that with a lightweight bumper style case just makes sense. But I do think this thing looks amazing on the Pro models this year. So, if you're the type of person who rarely ever drops their phone, then go for it. You won't be disappointed with the quality of the case itself. But just beware, the edges of this year's phone will get banged up pretty easily if they take a direct

title: iPhone 17 vs iPhone 17 Pro: Watch This Before You Buy or Upgrade
transcript: Hey everyone. So, here they are. On my left, I have the iPhone 17 in this really nice lavender color, and on my right, the iPhone 17 Pro in silver. I've been using both of these side by side for about over a week now. The Pro has been my main daily driver, and the base 17 has been my secondary phone, and I have to be honest, this year, the line between the base model and the Pro model has never been blurriier, which makes the decision of which one to buy a lot tougher. So, in this video, we're going to do a direct side-by-side comparison to help you figure that out. We'll look at the design and this whole scratchgate thing with the Pro, the displays, which are surprisingly similar this year, the performance and the Pro's secret weapon for cooling, and of course, the cameras. If you're thinking about buying one of these or maybe upgrading from an older iPhone, stick around. Let's figure this out together. All right, let's start with the design and how they feel in the hand. Side by side, you can see they share a similar DNA. Both have these incredibly thin bezels, which we'll get to in a minute, and a more rounded feel to the edges, which makes them really comfortable to hold, but the big difference is in the materials and the camera module. The iPhone 17 here has a familiar aluminum frame and a standard glass back. It feels great. It's what we expect from a base model iPhone. The Pro, however, has this new unibody aluminum design, and instead of a camera bump, it has this new camera plateau. Honestly, when I first saw the Pros design in pictures, I thought it was a bit weird, but after using it, I really love it. It makes the older Pro models feel kind of dated now. I don't know if it's just me, but I'm curious to hear what you guys think about it down in the comments. But, we have to talk about the issue with the Pro's new design, Scratchgate. You've probably seen the headlines because Apple switched from titanium back to aluminum for the Pro. There have been a lot of reports of the finish on the colored models, especially the cosmic orange and deep blue scratching and chipping very easily. Now, from what the experts are saying, it's not just that aluminum is a softer material. The issue seems to be the very sharp corners on this new camera plateau. The process that adds the color, called anodization, creates a protective layer that's just more brittle and not as strong on those hard edges. So, if it gets bumped, the color can flake off. This is exactly why I chose the silver model for my Pro. It's basically raw aluminum, so if it gets any minor scuffs, they'll be much harder to see. My advice is if you go for the Pro this year, you should probably plan on using a case. The standard 17 doesn't seem to have this issue because it's literally different design language. Now, let's talk about the screens because this is where things get really interesting this year. For the first time ever, both the base iPhone 17 and the iPhone 17 Pro have prootion displays. That's right, both of these screens can ramp all the way up to a super smooth 120 Hz and down to 1 Her Herz to save battery with the always on display. This is a massive, massive upgrade for the base model. For years, the 60 Hz screen was the biggest reason to spend more on a Pro model, and now that reason is gone. This one feature completely transforms the experience of using the base 17. It finally feels like a pro model. Every scroll, every animation, especially with the new liquid glass style in iOS 26, is just buttery smooth on both of these phones. The viewing experience is practically identical and is fantastic on both. Okay, so they both have amazing screens, but what's powering them? Well, the iPhone 17 has the new A19 chip and the 17 Pro has the A19 Pro. As you'd expect, both of them are incredibly fast. I ran Geekbench 6 on both. And you can see the scores right here. The 17 scored an insane 3,573 on single core and 9,143 on multi-core. The 17 Pro is a bit faster with 3,822 on single core and a massive 9,848 on multi-core. For perspective, that multi-core score on the Pro is higher than the M2 chip in an iPad Pro, a desktop level chip. But for everyday tasks, texting, browsing, social media, you're not going to notice a difference. They both fly. The real difference, and the Pro's secret weapon is how it handles that power over time. For the first time ever in an iPhone, the 17 Pro has a vapor chamber cooling system. In simple terms, it's a super efficient passive cooling system that spreads heat out incredibly quickly. This is why Apple went back to the aluminum body on the Pro. Aluminum is great at getting rid of heat, so it works with the vapor chamber to keep the phone cool. And this is a legitimate game changer. I felt it from the moment I started setting the Pro up. Usually, iPhones get pretty warm during that process, but this one stayed noticeably cool. Over the past week, I have never felt this phone get hot. The Base 17 can still get a bit warm under heavy load, but the Pro just stays cool. This means for things like long gaming sessions or shooting a lot of high-res video, the Pro can maintain its peak performance for much longer without slowing down. Okay, let's talk about one of the most important things for any phone, battery life. And in this comparison, there is a very, very clear winner. In standardized tests, the results are pretty stark. The base iPhone 17 lasted for about 12 hours and 47 minutes of continuous web surfing. That's okay. It's basically the same as last year's model, and it'll get most people through a day, but heavy users might find themselves reaching for a charger before bed. The iPhone 17 Pro, on the other hand, is in a different league. It lasted for a massive 15 hours and 32 minutes in the same test. In my week of using it as my main phone, the battery has been phenomenal. It's easily an all day phone, even with heavy use. And with lighter use, I could definitely stretch it to two days. So, why is there such a huge gap? Well, it comes down to three things. First, the Pro just has a physically larger battery. It got a huge 19% capacity increase over last year, while the B7 only got a small bump. Second, that A19 Pro chip is incredibly efficient, delivering more performance while using less power than its rivals. But the third reason is that vapor chamber we talked about. By keeping the phone cooler, it allows the chip to run more efficiently and it also protects the battery itself from heat which degrades it over time. This means the Pro not only lasts longer on a daily basis, but its battery health should also hold up better in the long run. So, if battery life is one of your top priorities, the decision is pretty simple. The iPhone 17 is adequate, but the iPhone 17 Pro is a true battery champion. All right, let's flip them over and talk cameras. And we'll start with the front camera because it's a new feature they both share. As you've probably seen online, the front camera on the entire 17 series is a huge upgrade. You can get this super wide landscape style shot for selfies or video calls without actually turning your phone sideways. It's fun, it's functional, and it's great to see on both models. The real difference is on the back. The iPhone 17 has a fantastic new dual 48 megapixel system. The photos are great. It's a solid setup. The iPhone 17 Pro also has a 40 megapixel main and ultra wide, but it adds a third lens, a 4x optical telephoto lens and can do a sensor crop that results in an 8x optical quality zoom. And that telephoto lens is the key difference. It means you can zoom into 4x and 8x and get a perfectly clear, sharp image. Whereas the base 17 has to use digital zoom, which is essentially just cropping the image. The Pro can also shoot video in 4K at 120 frames per second, which is incredible for slow motion. All right, so after using both side by side for a week, which one should you get? This year, the iPhone 17 is a truly fantastic phone. The biggest downside of pastbased models, that 60 Hz display is finally gone. If you want a phenomenal all-around phone that finally gives you a pro experience with a super fast chip and great cameras, the iPhone 17 is the one to get. For the vast majority of people, this is the best value and the smartest choice. But if you're a power user, a creator, or a serious mobile gamer, the iPhone 17 Pro is built for you. You should spend the extra money if you're someone who will really use that telephoto lens for photography, or if you're going to be pushing the phone to its limits with heavy tasks, where that advanced vapor chamber cooling will make a real difference in sustained performance. So, that's my take. The base 17 is so good this year. It's my default recommendation, but the Pro offers meaningful upgrades for those who truly need them. I'm really curious to hear what you all think. Let me know down in the comments which one you'd pick or if you have any questions. Thanks so much for watching and I'll catch you in the next

As for codecs, unfortunately nothing has changed, which I already pointed out when reviewing the third generation Airpods Pro.  Despite all this, it means that through AC with good quality headphones we get sound that will appeal to the overwhelming majority of people.  However, I must remind you of what I said in the Powers and Wilkins PX8s2 review. And remember that the iPhone codec also has a lot to say here. You will use about 70, maybe 75% of the capabilities of these headphones. The music suffers the most when listening at low volume, where microdynamics, those little musical things, are still audible when using the aptx lossless codec supported by selected Android phones.  And with the AAC connection there is much less of it. And that's it.  No offense.  We simply have to continue to reckon with this state of affairs .  And if that's a problem, seek happiness elsewhere.  And now the camera. Despite everything, despite this really cool square matrix on the front, the advantages of which I showed during my first impressions of the regular iPhone 17, and also despite the increased resolution of the telephoto sensor along with the modification of the focal length, which now gives four times zoom instead of five, which is a positive change because there is no such jump between the main lens and the telephoto lens, I still think that the term "status quo" best describes this camera system here as well. The iPhone still takes predictable photos, which are especially effective in nice weather , when shadows, clouds, sky and its color have to play with what we have in front of us. Then this phone absolutely delivers. I must honestly say that despite my complaints about this camera compared to the top Android offerings, when Apple TV recently showed me archived photos from two or three years ago taken with its predecessors, not always with the pro mark, it made me think and tone it down a bit, because even on these 55 inches these shots are nice and, above all, they can capture those moments without gnashing my teeth about their quality.  And I'm convinced this phone will be able to do that too.  Probably the best of them.  So treat every photo comparison as a simple attempt to make their perception more realistic in relation to the competition and perhaps also as a bit of an attempt to tear some things away from marketing. Zoom is good, but not outstanding.  It already abstracts from high digital values, which usually serve as a natural curiosity.  For me, the most important thing is what happens up to 10 times the value.  And in this field it is okay and nothing more.  Comparison with the predecessor often only shows a clear difference after a significant magnification. Of course, there will be some examples, for example at night, where the differences are a bit more noticeable.  However, this is not the rule. A comparison with competitors from outside the ecosystem shows the distance that this lens still maintains despite the increased parameters, and this applies not only to still life shots, but also to portraits, which, compared to iPhones, are slightly less noisy in the case of the current generation.  But when we include the Xiaomi 15 Ultra in this comparison , this distance can sometimes be embarrassing.  In terms of visuals and depth, the iPhone still takes photos that are quite flat.   I also have a problem with this 8x zoom and what it says about optics. I am still relatively able to accept the concept of optical quality zoom with the stops used on a 2x or 8x zoom, but there are also places in the product description that sometimes simplify and suddenly these values become pure optical zoom, and this is manipulation.  There are three optical focal lengths, three lenses, and the rest is marketing, which has somewhere conventionally defined when a digital zoom can be called lossless, i.e. of this optical quality. This is such a digression. The biggest advantage of the front camera is its flexibility, i.e. horizontal and vertical photos from any hand position. However, the comparison with the Pixel shows that it is difficult to come to a clear verdict whether this camera, apart from its innovative functionality, brings any new quality to the image itself.  The static shot actually gives the pike a slight advantage.  In turn, with video, the iPhone smoothes the image a bit, and the pixel sharpens it, but in terms of quality, in my opinion, it is comparable. I won't describe every photo and video, so I'll just turn up the music a bit and you can easily compare the subsequent shots, both static and in motion. As always, I'd like to leave you with some closure, but the status quo is definitely not enough.  This year, the regular 17 has a much greater power to attract Android users to the iOS side. That's without a doubt.  And I will also repeat that with the 16 Pro you should not fidget.   The Pro model remains the bastion of the third lens, which is better, but not by as much as people who compare it to the Oppo, Xiaomi or Vivo brands would expect.  In the Apple ecosystem this is progress, although I also consider it cosmetic, but outside of this bubble the progress is definitely too little.  At the same time, I appreciate things like increasing the minimum memory to 256 gigabytes and RAM to 12 gigabytes, because for people who change their phones once every three years, such values ​​will definitely make this phone responsive in the long run .  And for me, the value in itself is that it remains one of the few full-fledged flagships that is relatively normal in size.  Another value is the ability to record videos in a flat Log profile, which it cannot do.  for example, the Huawei Pura 80 Ultra, recently announced as the best photographic smartphone .  In photography, maybe so, but when it comes to videos, the iPhone still has its arguments.   I did n't say anything about the screen and I did it intentionally, because although on paper it is brighter and theoretically reflects less light, in everyday life it wasn't something that caught my eye and made me say that there is such an unequivocal organoleptic progress.  It wasn't like that. I will repeat, in my private life I stay with R. It is not the best.  I will say more, in terms of the price to the number of ingredients on the plate, it is the last item on the menu, but at the same time, for me, the least boring, the least devoid of emotions. Foreheads my dears.

I know I have the calendar here as well, but this doesn't give all the information that I want. So, I can just quickly view my calendar right here and then just hide it away again. title: iPhone 17 Pro Max - Call of Duty Mobile Gaming Test!

title: IPHONE 17 PRO MAX - КОРИЧНЕВАЯ МАГИЯ APPLE
transcript: This is the most boring phone I've reviewed in years.  It is generally accepted that Apple has an unspoken cycle where they first release a significantly updated iPhone, which brings in all sorts of innovations and revolutions, and then the following year they release a minor update that simply improves the characteristics of last year's iPhone.  And then the next year the cycle starts again.  A truly new revolutionary iPhone is coming out again.  So, to be honest , I get the feeling that Apple is simply stuck in this cycle at the second stage and can’t seem to restart it.  For the last 3 years, making videos about the iPhone has been torture for me.  I won’t even hide the fact that I spent a whole week, probably even more, just thinking about what to tell you about this phone so that it wouldn’t just be some kind of reading out of new specifications and numbers from the Apple website.  Every time I open the box of a new iPhone, I have the same desire: to whine about total ideological stagnation.  But I do n’t even want to do that anymore, because this innovative impotence of the Cupertino people has lasted so long that even whining about it is starting to get on everyone’s nerves.  The most popular, and one might even say mainstream, point of complaint among video bloggers about iPhones is the lack of that much- vaunted Apple magic you felt when unpacking new iPhones back in the days of Steve Jobs. But, you know, I don’t agree with this thesis .  I believe that the Apple magic has never really gone away.  It's just that now this concept means something different.  You've all probably seen this fragment from the presentation of the very first iPhone.  And you know, back then, just like now, many people thought that there was simply nothing particularly radical to change in smartphones.  There are buttons, there are styluses, and with their help the phones are controlled perfectly.  What else can you come up with here?  Similar opinions are still popular today.  For example, I get comments like these in defense of the poor Apple corporation when I write some posts about how the new iPhone is just a dull piece of crap without any innovation.  Many people think that if they can't think of anything that could be radically redesigned or added to the new iPhone, then everything has already been thought of, and there is nothing left to add or change .  You just need to churn out the same phone year after year.  But once upon a time, everyone thought the same about phones before the first iPhone came out.  And then Steve Jobs comes out and says: "We've invented multitouch, no buttons needed, no styluses needed, you'll do everything with your fingers ."  You need to understand that creating innovations is not your job as a user.  It's the job of the company that makes these smartphones, and ideally their job is to come up with something that you've never even thought of before .  Yes, as clichéd as it may sound, it’s true that back then, in the days of Steve Jobs, every new iPhone was at least a small, but still a revolution.  Every time you opened that little treasured box, you immediately had in your head a small list of what exactly you wanted to test, what you wanted to try with your own hands.  The box with this iPhone, still sealed, lay right in front of me on the table for three long weeks.  I bought this smartphone when I was leaving New York.  And I didn’t open it not because I needed to remove the unpacking or because I was afraid that I would leave some scratches on it. No, I just wasn't interested.  Even the previous two iPhones I reviewed, yes, they looked almost identical to each other, but I unboxed them and knew that I would at least try out some of the new buttons.  In the case of this iPhone, I looked at this box every day for three weeks and thought: "What should I do with it ? This is what I'm going to tell the audience about ."  Yes, they put in a new processor that is a little more powerful.  Yes, they put in a couple of new camera sensors, slightly increased the battery capacity and that's it.  All. Basically, I gave you a review of this iPhone.  Well, they also say that the glass on the display has become less reflective, but if I were looking at it now, I wouldn’t say so.  But if even this was enough to make you covet the new iPhone 17 Pro Max, don't rush to buy it, because you can win it simply by subscribing to the two channels in the description.  The first channel is JTID, a cult community of fans of games, movies, and other entertainment, and the second is Jvicha, where you can find out all the latest, most interesting, and relevant news from the world of IT.  Then, after 2 weeks, a winner will be randomly selected from among those who signed up using a special bot, whose name will appear in the same post in which the competition conditions are currently described. Shipping is free worldwide, so you can participate. Well, okay, let's go into more detail.   We 're focusing on the new design because it clearly demonstrates what this much-vaunted Apple magic is. This design was leaked long before the presentation, and, as we now understand, the accuracy of these leaks was 100%. And the reaction of most journalists, bloggers, and even ordinary users was, to put it mildly, not very positive. The presentation takes place, the design doesn't change one bit compared to what was leaked, and at the iPhone release, it's in short supply again.  The same people who were spitting at this design started saying that, well, actually, everything is not so bad.  I can still understand why the journalists' opinions changed.  Well, when you're invited to some cool presentation or someone sends money to your editorial office, it's clear that things can really change a bit for me, even in just a couple of months. But this time I sincerely don't think that's the case, because even ordinary people, who no one sent anything, no money, who no one invited to any presentations, even they lined up in huge lines, bought pre-orders for this phone, both on the Apple website and in other third-party stores.  And in the European Union, I'm in Italy now, and it's still impossible to buy an iPhone 17 Pro Max so that you can just go to the store today and pick it up.  No, pre-orders are still 3-4 weeks away.  And why is that so? Why, a few months ago, when this design first leaked, people reacted to it by saying that it was complete crap, that it was completely incomprehensible.  Several months pass, and the design does not change at all.  It turns out that this is really what it looks like.  And that's all.  The same people who were just criticizing him are now like, "Well, there's something to it."  And I don't know why this happens.  I don't have any rational, logical explanation for this, and that's why it seems to me that this is precisely what is known as Apple's magic today.  Replacing a titanium frame and glass back with an aluminum coffin with a glass door has consequences far beyond just aesthetics .  What is the reason Apple abandoned Titan anyway?  According to them , Titanium distributes heat very poorly , and if they made the same case out of Titanium, the processor would still constantly throttle, even with the vapor chamber .  But I have a question: why did you even make such a design if you have to give up titanium, which people really liked, and also add this ugly glass cutout for magnetic charging?  However, some publications doubt that the issue is really about heat distribution, pointing out that titanium cases are one dollar more expensive than aluminum ones.   It might seem like just a dollar, but when you consider the tens of millions of phones sold each year, that translates into savings of hundreds of millions of dollars over a few years. Moreover, after the sales started, it turned out that for many users, the aluminum part of the new case began to change color after just a few weeks.  And iPhones that were orange when purchased were massively transformed into pink.  But perhaps the new aluminum case will be justified at least by the fact that it is more scratch-resistant than glass.  No, here's a blogger who scratched an iPhone with a kitchen knife, and you can clearly see the scratches on the aluminum and the lack of scratches on the glass cutout.  Just a reminder that the 16 Pro Max had an entire back made of glass.  The situation is even worse with the ribs of the chamber block.  With them you won't even need a knife. They can be scratched by ordinary coins or keys, which can easily end up in the same pocket as the phone. So, the only thing that really matters is that the iPhone with the new design is that it scratches more.  If you think Apple couldn't have foreseen this problem, well, they could have, because something similar happened in the past with the original iPhone 5, which also had an aluminum case and was also prone to scratches, leading to a huge number of complaints.  And finally, there is such a thing as a feeling of premiumness.  Yes, when you pay a grand and a half for a phone, you have every right to want to feel that grand and a half every time you pick that phone up.  Again , this may be subjective, but to me the titanium frame felt somehow richer to the touch.  Well, titanium, in principle, is a more expensive metal.  And not just like that.  It is much stronger than aluminum.  The metal that made up the frame of this smartphone is used to make compressor disks for jet engines, for example, and the metal that made up the body of this smartphone is used to make 80-cent soda cans. By the way, it tastes better made from glass.  Yes, I understand that the comparison with a can is not entirely correct, because, well, after all, this is aluminum, it is anodized, but it is this anodization that is the reason why some orange smartphones turn pink.  And why can the ribs of the chamber block be scratched even by a coin in your pocket?  On this topic, I will also share with you my personal experience.  As you remember, this phone was supposed to be given away for a raffle, so I literally guarded it like the apple of my eye, carrying it around in my pocket all those days of filming without anything at all , without coins, and especially without keys.  And yet, on the last day, I take it out of my pocket and see this on it .  The paint chip is exactly in the same place I mentioned earlier, on the edge of the chamber block.  If you are still going to buy this phone, I advise you to immediately put it in a case. Now I have to sell it and buy a new one for the raffle.  This is despite the fact that my sixteenth iPhone with a titanium frame and glass back, after a year of carrying it in my pocket with everything possible, without a case, looks like new.  So what else should I tell you about this phone?  Okay, let's look at the cameras.  The main camera has not changed at all.  Well, that is, I take photos and they are absolutely identical, which is not surprising to me, because in terms of hardware, it is exactly the same camera.  But, apparently, there haven't been any major changes in the algorithms either , because, well, it's literally the same thing, pixel for pixel .  The TV camera, however, was perhaps the most important hardware update this year.  The sensor size has increased by 56%, and the resolution has increased fourfold to 48 pixels instead of the previous twelve. Thanks to this, the TV camera now has not only the main zoom, which has become fourfold, but also an eightfold zoom, which, according to Apple, is an optical-quality zoom.  But what does this optical quality mean?  In 4x zoom mode, you take a 12-megapixel photo with a 48-megapixel sensor.  This means that information from four pixels of the camera sensor will be collected and processed for one pixel of the photograph.  And in the eight-fold zoom mode, shooting will take place on the cropped central part of the sensor.  And so, in this mode, you take a 12-megapixel photo again, but not on the forty-eight-megapixel sensor, but on a twelve-megapixel section of this sensor.  And for every single pixel in a photograph, information will be collected not from four sensor pixels, but from just one.  This means more noise due to less information and more blur due to more aggressive use of noise reduction algorithms.  But this means that in the 8x zoom mode the image will be worse than in the 4x zoom mode.  But what if we compare the iPhone's 8x zoom with a similar zoom on another phone, which is achieved not by cropping the sensor like the iPhone, but by using a separate periscope camera, like the one on the Samsung S23 Ultra, which I happened to have with me. And while the first couple of photos showed a significant advantage for the Samsung, and I thought that a three-year-old phone would now give the new 17- year-old iPhone a run for its money, all the other photos showed very similar results in terms of detail.  And I must admit that, despite my initial skepticism, it can indeed be said that the iPhone's 8x zoom is an optical-quality zoom, because it really does produce photos on par with a dedicated 10x Samsung camera.  Several whole minutes of the presentation were devoted to the front camera.  It was reported how many selfies were taken last year.  And this prompted Apple to increase the front camera sensor.  And thanks to this, you will no longer have the problem that you and your friends cannot fit into the frame.  [laughter] Let's be honest, my channel subscribers never had this problem before.  Personally, I almost never use the front camera .  I am a serious person.  and how some streamers do cringe dances on the front camera.  I have never in my life And finally, the front glass of the new iPhone has become more resistant to scratches.  The chip in the new iPhone has become some percentage more powerful. Autonomy has been increased by approximately 15%.  And that's basically it.  Now you know absolutely everything there is to know about the new iPhone 17 Pro Max.
